metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default JWKS HTTP fetch timeout value (e.g. 10 s) and, if it is operator-configurable, add its config key name (`auth_jwt_jwks_timeout`?) and default to the flat-key table so AC 7 has a concrete value to assert against.
evidence: 'bounded HTTP timeout' in SSRF-safe JWKS fetch bullet — no value given
AC 7 observable oracle is 'a timeout is passed to the client' — no target value to assert against
Six other config keys each carry explicit defaults; timeout has none
findings.finding: The JWKS HTTP fetch timeout is called 'bounded' in Security and tested in AC 7, but the plan never states the default timeout value or whether it is a named config key — an executor must independently decide both.
findings.location: Security section ('bounded HTTP timeout'); AC 7 ('a timeout is passed to the client')
scenarios: Executor hardcodes an arbitrary timeout (e.g. 5s vs 30s) with no plan backing
AC 7 cannot be written as a deterministic assertion without knowing the expected timeout value
Two implementations by different agents produce incompatible defaults
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default 10) to the registered config keys list and update the Security bullet to read 'bounded HTTP timeout (default 10 s, controlled by `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout`)'; update AC 7's oracle to '…a timeout of `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` seconds is passed to the client'.

findings.finding: The plan declares a 'bounded HTTP timeout' for the JWKS fetch as a hard security requirement, but never specifies the default timeout value or its upper bound. The AC observable oracle is only 'a timeout is passed to the client', which passes even if the implementation chooses 300 seconds — effectively unbounded under load, enabling a slow-JWKS-server DoS that holds MCP request goroutines open.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' bullet; AC7 — 'a timeout is passed to the client'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The JWKS HTTP fetch timeout MUST default to ≤5 seconds (configurable via `auth_jwt_jwks_fetch_timeout`, no ceiling higher than 30 s).' Update AC7 to: 'a timeout ≤5 s is passed to the client (observable oracle: the value passed to PyJWKClient/urllib is ≤5 s).'

findings.finding: The `scp` scope-alias mapping is a named deliverable in the What section ("maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes") but no Acceptance Criterion explicitly requires testing the `scp` claim path. AC3 only says "expected scopes" without mandating a variant with the `scp` field, so a defective implementation that ignores `scp` would pass all ACs.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes') vs AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: 'Unit test asserts a JWT whose scopes are carried in `scp` (not `scope`) also yields an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes (observable oracle: returned `AccessToken.scopes` field).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the cooldown check-and-set is protected by an `asyncio.Lock` (or equivalent atomic mechanism) so the 'at most one JWKS fetch per cooldown window' invariant holds under concurrent async access; update the relevant AC to assert this under concurrent (not just sequential) burst.
evidence: 'the verifier caps refetch on an unknown kid to at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds (default 30) (implemented by the verifier itself, not PyJWKClient's lifespan)' — plan promises the invariant
ABSENCE: no mention of asyncio.Lock, threading.Lock, or atomic timestamp swap to protect the cooldown check-then-set
In an async server, multiple coroutines can interleave between the 'read last_refetch' and 'write last_refetch' steps
findings.finding: The cooldown tracker (last refetch timestamp) is shared mutable state that multiple concurrent async tasks may read-check-then-write simultaneously. The plan asserts 'at most one refetch per cooldown window' as a security property but does not specify atomic mutation (lock, CAS, or asyncio.Lock), leaving a TOCTOU race: two concurrent requests with distinct unknown kids can both observe that the cooldown has elapsed, both call into PyJWKClient, and both trigger a JWKS fetch — violating the stated security invariant.
findings.location: Security ('key rotation without DoS') / AC 'Unit test asserts unknown-kid cooldown: a burst of requests…triggers at most one JWKS fetch'
scenarios: Attacker sends burst of 100 requests each with a distinct unknown kid; if the cooldown check is unguarded, all 100 could pass the elapsed check simultaneously and trigger 100 JWKS fetches in the same window
Legitimate key rotation: two concurrent requests for the rotated key both trigger a refetch; double-fetches are benign but break the stated invariant and waste resources
findings.suggested_fix: Guard the cooldown block with an `asyncio.Lock`: `async with self._refetch_lock: if now - self._last_refetch > cooldown: self._last_refetch = now; <do fetch>`. This makes the check-then-act atomic within the async context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the unknown-kid cooldown wrapper uses an `asyncio.Lock` (or equivalent atomic gate such as an `asyncio.Event` for in-progress deduplication) so that concurrent coroutines cannot simultaneously pass the cooldown check and trigger multiple JWKS fetches within a single window.
evidence: 'a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time' — implies a simple timestamp read-compare-write, not an atomic operation
FastMCP is an async framework; multiple coroutines can concurrently execute the compare and both see an expired cooldown before either updates the timestamp

The AC specifies 'at most one' as the observable oracle, but the described mechanism cannot guarantee this under concurrency without explicit locking (e.g., asyncio.Lock or an asyncio.Event to signal in-progress fetch)
findings.finding: The 'thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time' for the unknown-kid cooldown is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) pattern that breaks under async concurrency: two coroutines can simultaneously read the timestamp, both find the cooldown elapsed, and both trigger a JWKS fetch within the same window — violating the plan's hard 'at most one refetch per cooldown window' guarantee.
findings.location: Approach & rationale ('implements the unknown-kid refetch cooldown itself (a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time)') / Security ('at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds') / AC 'a burst of requests with distinct unknown kids within one cooldown window triggers at most one JWKS fetch'
scenarios: Under moderate load, two requests with distinct unknown kids arrive simultaneously; both coroutines check `now - last_refetch >= cooldown`, both see True, both call the JWKS endpoint — doubling fetch rate and defeating the DoS guard
A slow IdP causes the JWKS fetch to take several seconds; more concurrent requests accumulate and all proceed past the cooldown check before the first fetch completes
findings.suggested_fix: Use an `asyncio.Lock` to guard the check-then-act: acquire the lock, re-check the cooldown (double-checked locking), perform the fetch if still needed, update the timestamp, release. Alternatively, track an `asyncio.Event` for 'fetch in progress' and have subsequent waiters await the event rather than triggering their own fetch.

findings.finding: The 'symmetric algorithm' category used in the startup-refusal rule is referenced as a security-critical enum but its membership is never enumerated — the plan gives only `HS256` as an example, leaving `HS384`, `HS512`, and other HMAC/symmetric alg IDs as implementer-defined.
findings.location: Security section: 'refuse to start if any symmetric algorithm (e.g. `HS256`) is configured'
findings.suggested_fix: In Security: 'Refuse to start if `auth_jwt_algorithms` contains any of: `HS256`, `HS384`, `HS512`, `none`, or any other non-asymmetric identifier not in {RS256, RS384, RS512, ES256, ES384, ES512, PS256, PS384, PS512}.'

findings.finding: The fallback for a malformed/partial JWKS HTTP response (e.g., HTTP 200 with invalid JSON, missing `keys` array, or a valid JWKS that simply does not contain the requested `kid`) is unspecified — the plan only handles 'fetch failure' (network/HTTP error).
findings.location: Security section: 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny); a definitively invalid token returns None'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `scope`/`scp` precedence and merge rule: specify which claim wins when both are present, what format each is expected in (string vs. array), and what value is returned when neither is present.
evidence: Plan states: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' with no further elaboration
No merge rule (union? prefer `scope`? prefer `scp`? reject token?) is specified for the dual-claim case
No fallback is specified for the case where neither claim is present (empty set? reject?)
findings.finding: The plan's `scope`/`scp`→scopes mapping rule references two competing JWT claim names but never defines the decision protocol for when both are simultaneously present in the payload — an executor must invent a merge/precedence rule.
findings.location: What section: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'
scenarios: A real-world Okta/Auth0 token may include both `scope` (space-delimited string) and `scp` (array) with different values — executor must guess which wins.
A token with neither claim produces undefined behavior (empty scopes vs. token rejection are both valid interpretations).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Approach section: 'When both `scope` (space-delimited string) and `scp` (array) are present, prefer `scope` (union both as a safety fallback, or prefer one — specify explicitly). When neither is present, treat scopes as the empty set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare in the Security section that TLS certificate verification MUST remain enabled on the JWKS fetch (no `ssl_verify=False`, no trust-all `SSLContext`), and add an AC asserting the PyJWKClient is constructed without cert-verification disabled.
evidence: Plan states: 'HTTPS-only (an http:// URI is rejected at construction), with a bounded HTTP timeout. Private/link-local hosts…are rejected by default' — but says nothing about certificate chain verification being mandatory.

PyJWT's PyJWKClient uses `requests` under the hood; `requests` verifies SSL by default, but the plan does not declare this invariant or prohibit callers/tests from disabling it, leaving the posture undeclared on a security-critical auth path.
findings.finding: The plan mandates HTTPS-only for the JWKS URI but never declares that TLS certificate verification MUST be enforced (i.e., that disabling SSL cert checking — e.g., `ssl_verify=False`, a custom `SSLContext` with no verification, or a trust-all proxy — is prohibited). On the critical key-distribution channel, a MITM with a self-signed cert would allow an attacker to substitute arbitrary public keys, completely bypassing the signature check.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' bullet; AC line 'Unit test asserts construction-time SSRF guards'
scenarios: A deployment behind a corporate MITM proxy with a custom CA, where an operator silently adds `ssl_verify=False` to make it work, turns the JWKS fetch into a key-substitution channel.
An attacker who can intercept network traffic and present a self-signed cert (if verification is off) can serve arbitrary JWKS keys and forge accepted tokens.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'TLS certificate verification is enforced on all JWKS fetches; the verifier MUST NOT pass `ssl_verify=False` or an equivalent no-verify SSL context to PyJWKClient or its underlying HTTP client.' Add an AC: 'Unit test asserts PyJWKClient is constructed with default SSL verification (or equivalent), and that attempting to disable cert verification raises or is rejected by the verifier's constructor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a clause to the Security section declaring that validation-failure log messages MUST NOT include decoded token payload claims (sub, jti, scope, etc.); at most the `kid` or a sanitised error code is permitted.
evidence: Plan declares the return-value contract (raise vs. None) but is silent on what, if anything, is logged when validation fails — an undeclared posture on data crossing the trust boundary.
Token claims (sub, jti, scope) are PII/sensitive metadata; logging them on rejection is a common implementation mistake that the plan's security contract should foreclose.
findings.finding: The plan's error-handling posture for failed token validation is declared structurally ('a definitively invalid token returns None') but does not specify what diagnostic data — if any — MAY be included in log messages or exception messages on the validation-failure path. Token header and claim values (sub, jti, client_id, scope, etc.) logged at DEBUG or ERROR level on the boundary-crossing path would constitute claim leakage into logs.
findings.location: Security section — last bullet ('A JWKS-fetch failure raises … a definitively invalid token returns None'); AC lines for rejection tests
scenarios: An implementation logs the decoded (but rejected) token payload at DEBUG level for diagnostics; a developer enables DEBUG logging in production to troubleshoot an auth failure and inadvertently persists PII claims from thousands of rejected tokens into a log aggregation system.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'On validation failure, log messages MUST NOT include decoded token claims (sub, jti, scope, email, etc.); only the error category (e.g., "expired", "bad_iss") and, optionally, the raw `kid` from the header (which is public) may be logged. The full token string MUST NOT be logged at any level.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) that explicitly asserts a JWT carrying `scp` (rather than `scope`) produces an `AccessToken` with the correctly parsed scopes, so the `scope`/`scp` normalization described in What is verifiably covered.
evidence: What section: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'
AC3: 'a JWT with correct `iss`/`aud`(=resource) → an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes' — does not call out scp variant
No other AC references scp
findings.finding: The `scope`/`scp` dual-claim normalization is described as a deliverable in the What section but no Acceptance Criterion explicitly tests the `scp` variant of the scope claim — leaving a described feature without a covering test oracle.
findings.location: What section ('maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'); AC line 3 (only says 'expected scopes' without specifying scp)', no other AC covers it
scenarios: An IdP that issues `scp` (space-separated) instead of `scope` (array) — e.g. Azure AD / Entra ID — would not be covered by the existing test oracle; a regression in the scp normalization path would go undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: 'Unit test asserts a JWT with the `scp` claim (e.g. scp="read write") produces an AccessToken with scopes=["read","write"], in addition to the `scope` array variant.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the last-refetch timestamp is stored as instance state on `JWKSTokenVerifier` (not process-global, not class-level), and document this constraint explicitly in the Approach section.
evidence: Plan says: 'this verifier implements the unknown-`kid` refetch cooldown itself (a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time)'
No specification of whether the last-refetch timestamp is instance-level (per `JWKSTokenVerifier` object) or process-global
The AC (AC9) tests the cooldown behavior but does not constrain the state's placement scope
findings.finding: The cooldown state ('a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time') is described without specifying its placement scope — per-`JWKSTokenVerifier` instance vs. shared across instances — leaving the counter/state-increment placement ambiguous.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'a thin wrapper tracking the last refetch time'
scenarios: If multiple `JWKSTokenVerifier` instances are created (e.g. in test teardown/setup, or by a composite verifier), per-instance state means the cooldown window resets on each new instance — a test or production config that recreates the verifier per-request bypasses the flood guard entirely.
If process-global, two verifiers pointing at different JWKS URIs share a single cooldown counter, incorrectly throttling legitimate refetches for the second URI.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The last-refetch timestamp is an instance attribute of `JWKSTokenVerifier`, initialized at construction time, so each verifier instance maintains its own independent cooldown window relative to its own JWKS URI.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry documenting (a) why the verifier implements its own `kid`-flood cooldown instead of relying on `PyJWKClient`'s `lifespan` TTL, and (b) the threat model and design choices behind the SSRF-guard (HTTPS-only, private-host rejection, fixed-at-construction `jwks_uri`).
evidence: "PyJWKClient's lifespan is a cache TTL, not an unknown-kid flood guard, so this verifier implements the unknown-kid refetch cooldown itself" — rationale exists only in story prose, not in a durable design artifact
Plan says 'This story registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json + regenerates the reference docs' — no mention of ADR or CLAUDE.md update
T5e(c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update'
findings.finding: The novel security-architecture decisions — specifically the custom unknown-`kid` refetch cooldown (explicitly not delegated to PyJWKClient's `lifespan`) and the SSRF-guard design (HTTPS-only, fixed-at-construction, private-host rejection logic) — are explained inline in the story plan but the plan does not mention capturing them in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc. Future maintainers encountering the code will not have a durable record of WHY these choices were made rather than using the library's own facilities.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Security section ('Key rotation without DoS', 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch')
scenarios: A future maintainer sees the bespoke cooldown wrapper and PyJWKClient usage without understanding why PyJWKClient alone was insufficient — removes the cooldown wrapper thinking it's redundant
A security reviewer auditing the SSRF guard cannot find the threat model that justified the private-host-rejection-by-default policy
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/XXX-jwks-verifier-security-design.md` covering: (1) kid-flood-cooldown rationale (lifespan is a cache TTL not a flood guard), (2) SSRF guard threat model (jku/x5u injection, DNS rebinding, metadata endpoint exfiltration), (3) typ-check default-off rationale (provider variance). Reference it from CLAUDE.md's security section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a one-line inline recommendation to the `auth_jwt_expected_typ` reference-doc entry in S3 itself (e.g. 'Set to `at+JWT` for RFC 9068-compliant IdPs such as Keycloak/Okta; omit only if your IdP does not stamp `typ` on access tokens') rather than deferring all guidance to S7.
evidence: "S7 documents that operators SHOULD set auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT for RFC 9068-compliant IdPs" — documentation deferred to another story
AC: 'appear in the regenerated reference docs' — the key will appear but without the SHOULD recommendation
The plan itself notes this check 'defaults unset... because many IdPs do not stamp typ on access tokens' — nuanced trade-off operators need explained at the point of the config key
findings.finding: The plan defers the operator-facing security recommendation for `auth_jwt_expected_typ` ('operators SHOULD set `auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT` for RFC 9068-compliant IdPs') entirely to a future story (S7). The config key will ship in regenerated reference docs in S3 with no inline guidance, meaning operators reading the docs have no indication of when or why to set it. If S7 is delayed or de-scoped, this security-critical recommendation is never documented alongside the feature it governs.
findings.location: Security section ('Cross-JWT-confusion `typ` check'); AC line 2 (reference docs); S7 reference
scenarios: Operator deploys S3 without S7, reads reference docs, sees `auth_jwt_expected_typ` with no guidance, leaves it unset even for an RFC 9068 IdP (Keycloak, etc.) — missing a meaningful cross-JWT-confusion mitigation
S7 is de-scoped; the recommendation is never published
findings.suggested_fix: In the `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` registration for `auth_jwt_expected_typ`, include a `description` or `doc` field containing the SHOULD recommendation. This is a one-line addition that makes S3 self-contained regardless of S7's status.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the synchronous `PyJWKClient` JWKS fetch is offloaded with `run_in_executor` (or replaced with an async client) so it does not block the async event loop on cache misses; also specify the concrete HTTP timeout value (e.g. 5 s) as part of the done-definition.
evidence: 'Use PyJWT's PyJWKClient (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + kid lookup)' — PyJWKClient is synchronous
Plan does not mention run_in_executor or any async-safe fetch path
FastMCP is an async framework; blocking the event loop degrades all concurrent requests, not just the one waiting for JWKS
findings.finding: PyJWKClient issues synchronous HTTP requests. If `JWKSTokenVerifier.verify()` is awaited from an async handler (FastMCP is async), every JWKS cache miss blocks the event loop for the full network round-trip, stalling all concurrent requests on that worker. The plan mandates a 'bounded HTTP timeout' but does not specify the value or require the blocking call to be offloaded via `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor` (or an async JWKS client).
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Security ('bounded HTTP timeout') / AC 'a timeout is passed to the client'
scenarios: A JWKS cache miss (cold start, first request after TTL expiry, or cooldown-gated refetch) blocks the entire event loop for the network RTT to the IdP
Under moderate concurrency this serialises all requests at every JWKS refresh point
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the PyJWKClient fetch call in `await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, jwks_client.get_signing_key_from_jwt, token)` and add an AC asserting the timeout is ≤ a specific value (e.g. 5 s); alternatively, note in the plan that this verifier is always called via an executor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: JWKS fetch failures, JWT validation rejections (with failure category: expired/wrong-iss/wrong-aud/bad-sig/typ-mismatch), and cooldown-gate activations MUST emit a structured log entry (at WARN or ERROR level) so operators can distinguish IdP outage from invalid tokens.
evidence: 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny)' — plan describes the control flow but no logging or metric
'a definitively invalid token returns None' — same: no instrumentation
The T5b checklist requires 'New failure points instrumented with a metric/log/trace/alert'
ABSENCE: no mention of logging, structured events, or metrics anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (logging, metrics, or structured events) for JWKS fetch failures, JWT validation rejections, or cooldown activations. Operators have no signal to distinguish 'JWKS endpoint is down' from 'tokens are invalid' — both produce a 401, but the remediation is entirely different.
findings.location: Security section / AC 'Unit test asserts a JWKS fetch failure raises'
scenarios: JWKS endpoint goes down: all auth fails with 401; no alert fires; operators see user complaints but no server-side signal
kid rotation happens and cooldown delays refetch: legitimate tokens fail for up to 30 s; no log entry explains why

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` and `docs/env-vars.md` to the list of artifacts to update (alongside `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`), and confirm the `auth_jwt_*` env reads use a pattern the AST scanner recognizes (direct `os.environ.get` or a registered `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` shim).
evidence: scripts/gen_env_registry.py scans src/rebar/**/*.py for os.environ reads and writes docs/env-vars.md; a CI gate in test.yml:260 and gerrit-verify.yaml:219 fails the build if the file is stale
The new _mcp_auth.py will contain os.environ.get('AUTH_JWT_JWKS_URI', ...) and similar direct reads that the AST scanner will detect
The plan text enumerates MCP_ENV_VARS, server.json, scripts/check_server_manifest.py, and gen_mcp_reference.py but never mentions gen_env_registry.py or docs/env-vars.md
ABSENCE: no reference to 'gen_env_registry' or 'env-vars.md' anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: The `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` scanner and its CI drift gate for `docs/env-vars.md` are unenumerated consumers of the new `auth_jwt_*` environment variables that `_mcp_auth.py` will read. Both `test.yml` and `gerrit-verify.yaml` run `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py --check` and fail the build if `docs/env-vars.md` is stale. The plan names `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`/`scripts/check_server_manifest.py` and `gen_mcp_reference.py` but makes no mention of `gen_env_registry.py` or `docs/env-vars.md`, meaning an implementer following the plan exactly will leave this drift gate red.
findings.location: AC2 / 'This story registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json + regenerates the reference docs'
scenarios: An implementer adds AUTH_JWT_* os.environ reads in _mcp_auth.py; the gen_env_registry.py drift gate in CI then fails because docs/env-vars.md is stale, blocking the PR even though all other ACs pass.

If the new keys are read through a novel helper not in KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS, the scanner silently misses them and the drift gate passes but the registry is incomplete — the plan gives no guidance on which read pattern to use.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, append: 'and `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` regenerates `docs/env-vars.md`; read the `auth_jwt_*` vars via direct `os.environ.get("AUTH_JWT_…")` calls so the AST scanner picks them up automatically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the verifier handles JWTs without a `kid` header — either document that `kid` is required (and enforce/log it clearly) or specify the fallback behavior (e.g., attempt single-key JWKS match if exactly one key is present), and add an AC or test asserting the chosen behavior.
evidence: PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt parses the token header to extract `kid`; if `kid` is absent it either raises or attempts a heuristic single-key fallback depending on version
Plan is silent on kid-less token handling — no mention in What, Security, or any AC
Some real OIDC providers (early Keycloak configs, some Google service account tokens, single-key JWKS sets) issue JWTs without kid
findings.finding: The plan's mechanism uses `PyJWKClient` to look up the signing key by `kid` header, but does not address JWTs that lack a `kid` header — a valid and common case (RFC 7517 §4.5 permits omitting `kid` when a single key is unambiguous). PyJWKClient raises on a missing `kid`, and the plan's 'fetch failure raises / invalid token returns None' distinction does not map to this case, leaving the verifier's behavior undefined for `kid`-less tokens.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Security ('A JWKS-fetch failure raises … a definitively invalid token returns None')
scenarios: An operator configures a Keycloak instance that does not stamp `kid` on access tokens; every verification attempt raises an unexpected exception rather than returning None, breaking the composite verifier's deny-not-crash contract
During key rotation a provider briefly issues tokens without `kid` to ease the transition; these are silently treated as fetch errors rather than invalid tokens

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the PyJWKClient JWKS fetch is wrapped in `run_in_executor` (or equivalent) to avoid blocking the async event loop, OR document that this verifier always executes the fetch in a thread pool — and add the concrete HTTP timeout value as a done-definition requirement.
evidence: PyJWKClient is synchronous; it does not provide an async interface
Plan specifies 'bounded HTTP timeout' but not the mechanism for making the blocking call safe in an async context
FastMCP is an async framework; all request handlers run in an event loop — a blocking urllib call on the hot path blocks the loop
findings.finding: PyJWKClient performs synchronous blocking HTTP requests (via urllib) to fetch the JWKS endpoint. The plan does not specify that this call is offloaded from the async event loop (e.g., via `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor`). In the async FastMCP runtime, every JWKS fetch — cold start, post-cooldown refetch, TTL expiry — will block the entire event loop for the network round-trip, stalling all concurrent request handling. The data-flow as described is incomplete for the async runtime.
findings.location: Approach & rationale ('Use PyJWT's PyJWKClient (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + kid lookup)') / Security ('bounded HTTP timeout')
scenarios: Cold start: first request triggers JWKS fetch; all other concurrent requests stall until the fetch completes
After cooldown window: a request with an unknown kid triggers refetch; all in-flight requests at that moment stall for the IdP network RTT
Slow IdP (e.g., 3 s response time with a 5 s timeout): every JWKS miss stalls the event loop for up to 5 s, during which no other MCP tool calls can be served
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the PyJWKClient call in `await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, jwks_client.get_signing_key_from_jwt, token)` and note the concrete timeout value (e.g. 5 s) in the plan. Alternatively, note in the Approach section that the verifier schedules all JWKS I/O via the executor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve whether `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` must be wrapped with `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor` (or `anyio.to_thread.run_sync`) when called from the async `verify()` method, and add either the wrapper or an explicit rationale (e.g. 'JWKS fetches are rare; blocking is acceptable') to the plan.
evidence: Plan states: 'Use PyJWT's PyJWKClient (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + kid lookup) and jwt.decode'
Plan states 'bounded HTTP timeout' as a security property — but PyJWKClient's HTTP is synchronous; calling it from an async handler blocks the event loop, not just the timeout window
FastMCP is an async ASGI framework; all verifier calls are awaited from async request handlers
Plan never mentions asyncio.run_in_executor, anyio.to_thread.run_sync, or any async wrapper for the synchronous PyJWKClient
findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` (and the underlying JWKS fetch) issues synchronous blocking HTTP using `requests`/`urllib`. The plan places this client directly inside the `JWKSTokenVerifier` without addressing the async/sync impedance: every JWKS cache miss on an unknown `kid` will block the FastMCP async event loop for the full network round-trip duration, defeating the 'bounded HTTP timeout' safety property and introducing latency spikes under concurrency.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale section; Security section ('bounded HTTP timeout'); AC covering unknown-kid cooldown
scenarios: Under concurrent MCP requests, a single JWKS cache miss blocks the entire event loop while the HTTP fetch completes, serializing all in-flight requests
An attacker sending distinct unknown kid values (within the cooldown window) causes one blocking fetch but still fully occupies the event loop thread during that fetch
The 'bounded HTTP timeout' guard only limits how long the block lasts, not that blocking occurs — which may violate the async server's latency SLA
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach & Rationale section, add: 'JWKS fetches (cache misses) are dispatched via `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, ...)` or `anyio.to_thread.run_sync(...)` to avoid blocking the FastMCP event loop. Key-cache hits (the common case) are in-process dict lookups and do not block.' Alternatively, explicitly justify why blocking is acceptable for this verifier's expected traffic pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the sync/async impedance: the plan must either (a) name the async bridge (e.g. `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, client.get_signing_key_from_jwt, token)` or `anyio.to_thread.run_sync`) that wraps PyJWKClient's blocking HTTP call, or (b) justify why the blocking call is acceptable in the FastMCP async context (e.g. if FastMCP runs verifiers in a thread pool by design).
evidence: Partially-verified: `requests` is UNRESOLVED in the installed venv, consistent with PyJWT using urllib directly for synchronous HTTP in newer versions

Prior plan-review cache at .rebar/cache/plan-review/94c4-c391-3fed-4773/616c3bb41c61e2112ee3.json confirms: 'PyJWT's PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt() (and the underlying JWKS fetch) issues synchronous blocking HTTP'
The plan never mentions asyncio.run_in_executor, anyio.to_thread.run_sync, or any async wrapper for PyJWKClient
FastMCP is an async ASGI framework; all token verifier calls are awaited from async request handlers
findings.finding: PyJWKClient's JWKS HTTP fetch is synchronous (urllib-based). The plan places PyJWKClient directly inside JWKSTokenVerifier without describing how the sync HTTP call is lifted into the async FastMCP event loop. Every JWKS cache miss (including the unknown-kid cooldown refetch) will block the entire async event loop for the network round-trip duration — the plan's 'bounded HTTP timeout' property limits the duration of the block but does not eliminate it.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale — 'Use PyJWT's PyJWKClient (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + kid lookup)'; Security — 'bounded HTTP timeout'; AC: unknown-kid cooldown unit test
scenarios: Under concurrent MCP requests, a JWKS cache miss blocks the event loop during the HTTP fetch, serializing all in-flight requests for the fetch duration
An attacker sends unknown kid values; the cooldown prevents multiple fetches but the one allowed fetch still fully occupies the event loop thread
The integration test (httpx ASGITransport) may not catch this because it is single-threaded and does not exercise concurrent async behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Because PyJWKClient issues synchronous HTTP, the JWKS fetch (get_signing_key_from_jwt) is wrapped via `await anyio.to_thread.run_sync(client.get_signing_key_from_jwt, token)` so the event loop is not blocked during cache misses.' Verify that anyio is already available as a transitive of mcp (it is the async primitive FastMCP uses).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a response-size cap (e.g., refuse JWKS bodies > a documented maximum, such as 1 MB) to the JWKS fetch in the Security section, and add an AC asserting the verifier rejects oversized JWKS responses rather than consuming them.
evidence: Plan specifies 'bounded HTTP timeout' only; no mention of maximum JWKS response body size.
PyJWT's PyJWKClient streams via `requests` with no default response-size cap; the plan adds no guard on top.
The JWKS fetch is on the hot path for every token presented by a lower-trust caller, making memory exhaustion a realistic DoS vector.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bounded HTTP timeout for the JWKS fetch but does not specify a response-size limit. A large (e.g., multi-megabyte) JWKS response — from a misconfigured or hostile JWKS endpoint — could exhaust server memory. The timeout prevents slow reads but not fast-but-huge payloads.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch' bullet ('with a bounded HTTP timeout')
scenarios: An attacker who can influence the JWKS endpoint (e.g., a compromised IdP, a misconfigured `auth_jwt_jwks_uri`, or a DNS-spoofed private host after opting in with `allow_private_jwks_host=true`) returns a 512 MB JSON body, causing the verifier process to OOM on every token validation burst.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The JWKS HTTP response body is capped at a maximum size (e.g., 1 MB); a response exceeding this limit is treated as a fetch failure (raises, → composite deny).' Surface this as a named config key (e.g., `auth_jwt_jwks_max_response_bytes`, default 1 MB) or as a hardcoded internal constant documented in the section.

findings.finding: The `docs/env-vars.md` drift gate (`scripts/gen_env_registry.py`) is an unenumerated consumer: it AST-scans `src/rebar/*.py` for `os.environ` reads and auto-generates `docs/env-vars.md`, with a CI gate (in both `test.yml` and `gerrit-verify.yaml`) that fails on any diff. The new `auth_jwt_*` flat config keys will almost certainly be read via `os.environ` calls in `_mcp_auth.py`, causing this gate to flag drift. The plan names `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` and 'reference docs' but never mentions `docs/env-vars.md` or running `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py`.
findings.location: AC: 'registered in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, appear in the regenerated reference docs'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC bullet that reads 'registered in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, appear in the regenerated reference docs', append: 'and `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` is run to update `docs/env-vars.md` (env-var registry drift gate).'  Also add a Scope line: edit `docs/env-vars.md` (regenerated, not hand-edited).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by inspecting `mcp>=1.9`'s published dependency metadata) that PyJWT is a guaranteed transitive of all supported `mcp` versions; if not confirmed, add `pyjwt[crypto]` as an explicit entry in the `[mcp]` extra rather than relying on the transitive.
evidence: Plan text: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`; `pyjwt[crypto]` makes the `cryptography` backend explicit for RS/ES verification.'
pyproject.toml [mcp] extra: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` — no explicit pyjwt or cryptography pin.
jwt module IS installed in the venv (resolve_symbol confirmed), but the repo files do not establish it as a transitive of mcp>=1.9 specifically.
Absence: no `pyjwt` in pyproject.toml dependencies or extras currently.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' to justify that `pyjwt[crypto]` is a backward-compatible add to the `[mcp]` extra. The `pyproject.toml` `[mcp]` extra lists only `mcp>=1.9`; whether the installed `mcp>=1.9` SDK actually pulls in PyJWT as a transitive dependency is an empirically-checkable claim that cannot be confirmed from repo files alone. If the transitive is absent in some mcp versions, the `[mcp]` extra could ship without PyJWT, silently breaking the verifier at import time in environments that pin mcp to a version without that transitive.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`'; also Compatibility section
scenarios: An environment pins `mcp` to a version that dropped the PyJWT transitive (mcp's own deps may change across minor versions) — the verifier import fails silently.
A user installs only `nava-rebar[mcp]` expecting JWT verification to work, but the transitive is absent in their mcp version.
findings.suggested_fix: In `pyproject.toml`, change `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` to `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "pyjwt[crypto]>=2.8"]`. This makes the dependency explicit and idempotent whether or not mcp carries PyJWT transitively, and matches the plan's AC-1 intent anyway.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the installed `mcp>=1.9` SDK exposes a `JWTVerifier` or equivalent auth integration point (grep the installed mcp package); if it does not, remove or correct the 'same shape as FastMCP's JWTVerifier' rationale and confirm the custom verifier approach is the only available path.
evidence: Plan text: 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses'
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
search_files('JWTVerifier') → no matches anywhere in the repo
findings.finding: The plan states 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' as a design-similarity rationale for the PyJWT approach. `JWTVerifier` is not resolvable in the installed `mcp` / `mcp.server.fastmcp` packages (resolve_symbol returned UNRESOLVED), and no such symbol appears anywhere in the repo. This is an unverifiable empirical claim about FastMCP's internals used to justify the implementation shape.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small module on the SDK seam. PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`… the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses'
scenarios: FastMCP does not expose a `JWTVerifier` in the installed version — the shape-similarity rationale is baseless, and the implementation approach may diverge from any SDK-blessed pattern in ways that cause integration issues.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' to justify the backward-compatibility of adding `pyjwt[crypto]` to the `[mcp]` extra, but this claim is unverified: `pyproject.toml`'s `[mcp]` extra lists only `mcp>=1.9` with no version ceiling, and whether every version in that range carries PyJWT as a transitive is not confirmed from repo files or lock files.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale — 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`'; Compatibility — 'The `pyjwt[crypto]` add is backward-compatible; the SDK pin already lives in S1'

An operator pins `mcp` to a specific older version where PyJWT was not yet a transitive — the verifier import fails at startup with an ImportError

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
